title: Methodology
text:
NoteExecutive summary



This site is a Quarto static-content layer plus an embedded Shiny planner, with daily-refreshed UBC Okanagan course data sourced from the UBC Okanagan Academic Calendar and curated designation mappings sourced from the regulatory bodies’ published standards. The full data pipeline is open and reproducible from the project source repository; this page documents the architecture, the sources, and the refresh cadences in detail.



Architecture
The site has three coupled layers:

Static Quarto site, all content pages (course catalogue, designation pathways, learning outcomes, skills scaffold, methodology, announcements). Authored in markdown / qmd; rendered to HTML and served as static files.
Shiny degree planner, the interactive component, embedded as an iframe in the static site. Lets a student drag courses into year slots, pick a designation goal, and see real-time gap analysis.
targets data pipeline, daily scrape of the UBCO Academic Calendar, normalisation to a tidy parquet, plus the curated designation-mapping YAMLs. Output artefacts feed both the Quarto site (via embedded chunks) and the Shiny planner.



Data sources and refresh cadence









Feed
Source URL
How
Cadence




Course descriptions
okanagan.calendar.ubc.ca/course-descriptions/subject/<code>
rvest scrape, per-subject
Daily, 12:00 UTC


Program structure
okanagan.calendar.ubc.ca/.../freshwater-science
rvest scrape
Daily, 12:00 UTC


Course schedule (offered next year?)
UBCO Workday Student / SSC
TBD, scrape vs. API
Daily, 12:00 UTC


BCIA PAg UBCO-approved list
bcia.com/sites/.../UBCO Approved Courses_*.pdf
Manual OCR + curated YAML
Triggered on PDF update (BCIA edits the file ~annually)


CAB RPBio Credentialing Standard
cab-bc.org/wp-content/uploads/Credentialing_Standard_*.pdf
Manual OCR + curated YAML
Triggered on standard update


Program learning outcomes
Faculty of Science / EESC departmental docs
Manual transcription
Annual


Course learning outcomes
Instructor interviews
Manual collection
Per-course, on rolling basis





Scrape policy
UBCO’s calendar is publicly accessible. The scraper respects the following constraints:

Single sequential scrape (no parallel requests) with a polite User-Agent: limnology.ca UBCO Freshwater Pathways v0.x.
Per-request delay of 1 second between subject pages.
Local cache (file mtime check) so a re-run within the same day is a no-op.
All scraping happens server-side in GitHub Actions, never in the user’s browser.

If UBCO’s registrar office can provide a data feed or API, the scraper will be retired in favour of that.


Designation-mapping authority
The PAg and RPBio mappings are not scraped. The source documents (BCIA UBCO list, CAB Credentialing Standard) are PDF publications by the respective regulatory bodies. The workflow:

Download the latest PDF when published.
OCR locally to a text file in docs/reference/.
Hand-curate the UBCO course-code -> designation-category map into data/designation_mappings/pag.yml and .../rpbio.yml.
Versioned, dated YAMLs so historical pathways are preserved.

This preserves traceability: every course-counts-for-X claim on the site is backed by a citable, dated source.


Pipeline reproducibility
The targets pipeline plus the GitHub Actions cron workflow give:

Full provenance for every artefact (which scrape run, which source version, which curator).
Deterministic re-renders.
Public commit history of when designation mappings changed.



Stack inventory







Component
Library




Site
Quarto 1.6+


Scraping
rvest, httr2, xml2


Tidying
dplyr, tidyr, stringr, lubridate


Storage
arrow (parquet)


Pipeline
targets


Planner
shiny, bslib, DT, visNetwork (prereq graph)


Validation
assertr, testthat


Deploy
GitHub Actions, shinyapps.io (planner), limnology.ca (static site)

title: Interactive Degree Planner
text:
TipThe planner is live



Open the interactive planner in a new tab: obwb.shinyapps.io/ubco-freshwater-planner/
The app reads the live UBCO course catalogue and the PAg + RPBio designation mappings curated on this site. Tick the courses you’ve completed (or transferred in), pick one or both designation goals, and the app reports:

credits + degree-threshold check (120 / 78 Science / 42 upper / 36 upper Science)
PAg category coverage (8 foundational + 20 agrology with at least 8 senior)
RPBio category coverage (6 core areas + 5 three-of-five areas) plus the 13-biology count

This is v1: it’s intentionally simple (no drag-drop year slots yet, no prereq DAG viz). v2 will add those features once the v1 has been in front of students for a term.



What it will do
When live, the planner will let you:

Drag courses into year slots (Year 1, 2, 3, 4 and post-Year-4 summer).
Toggle designation goals (PAg, RPBio, neither, both).
See live gap analysis:

Which prerequisites are missing for the courses you have chosen.
Which BCIA / CAB category requirements are still unfilled.
Whether your total credits clear the 120 / 78 Science / 42 upper-level / 36 upper-level-Science thresholds.

View the prerequisite graph for your selected courses (visNetwork).
Export your plan as a PDF or text summary you can share with the program advisor.



In the meantime

Use the course catalogue for the year-by-year course list and prerequisite notes.
Use the PAg pathway and RPBio pathway for the curated 4-year sample plans + designation-fit narrative.
Use the Complementary pathways page for Chemistry, Biology, and hydrology/water-resources bundles.



Want to help shape it?
If you are a Freshwater Science student or instructor and want input into how the planner should behave, what gap signals matter most, what visual encoding is most useful, how aggressively it should flag prerequisite conflicts, please drop a note to Nelson.Jatel@UBC.ca or open a GitHub issue at the project source repository.

title: Program Learning Outcomes
text:
The current authority for Program Learning Outcomes (PLOs) is the v2 PLO set finalised on 28 April 2026 by Dr. Nelson Jatel, PAg, Lecturer and Program Advisor (memo: docs/reference/learning-outcomes/UBCO_Memo_PLOs_v2_20260428.docx). The v2 set consolidated the v1 draft (14 outcomes) down to 8 outcomes in 4 categories, based on:

Student focus-group feedback (Freshwater Science majors),
Faculty working-session input, and
Best-practice benchmarking against Penn State, Washington State, CSUSB, SUNY, U Michigan-Dearborn, and comparator BSc Freshwater programs at UW-Milwaukee, Western Michigan, and UW-Madison.

The 5-to-7-outcome range is the sector norm; v1’s 14 was flagged by both faculty and benchmarks as too long for meaningful assessment.

The eight v2 outcomes


Depth and Breadth of Knowledge

1. Explain water processes in the context of planetary cycles, surface processes, and environmental systems, including human alterations to freshwater ecosystems.


2. Describe the physical, chemical, and biological structure and function of freshwater systems.



Application of Knowledge

3. Collect, organise, and analyse water and environmental science data using appropriate field, laboratory, computational (e.g., R, Python), and statistical techniques, and represent results in appropriate graphical formats.


4. Apply the scientific method and quantitative reasoning to develop conceptual models, test hypotheses, and evaluate trade-offs in freshwater management decisions.


5. Acquire, critically assess, and synthesise freshwater science literature, Indigenous knowledge systems (ways of knowing), data sources, and spatial resources (e.g., maps, remotely sensed data) to inform interpretation and decision-making.


6. Develop water-related policy options and communicate them effectively to advance uptake by decision-makers.



Communication

7. Communicate limnology and environmental concepts to diverse audiences through formal written reports, oral presentations, technical posters, illustrations, and clear field, laboratory, and code documentation.



Professional Conduct

8. Demonstrate professional ethics, safe field and laboratory practice, effective teamwork, and respectful engagement with Indigenous Peoples, lands, and rights-holders.





How each course contributes, the I / R / M methodology
The curriculum-map methodology used to trace these PLOs through the program uses three coding labels for each course-learning-outcome (CLO) → PLO link:







Code
Meaning




I
Introductory, the course introduces the concept; students encounter it for the first time.


R
Reinforcement, the course practises the concept; students extend prior exposure.


M
Mastery, the course expects students to demonstrate the concept independently.



Combined with an X marker showing which CLO maps to which PLO, this is the same framework UBC’s Faculty of Science uses for the EECB program (Ecology, Evolution, and Conservation Biology) and other B.Sc. programs.






NotePilot: EESC 301 Limnology



A worked example of the methodology for EESC 301 (Limnology) mapped to the EECB program PLOs is committed at docs/reference/learning-outcomes/EESC 301 - Mapping to EECB Program Learning Outcomes - NJ.xlsx. It captures six course learning outcomes, links each to one or more program PLOs, and codes the depth (I / R / M). This is the template that needs to be replicated for every Freshwater Science required and elective course.




Curriculum map, pending build
Following the v2 PLO finalisation memo (§7 Recommended Next Steps), the next deliverable is the full curriculum map: every required Freshwater Science course mapped against the 8 PLOs with I / R / M coding. This page will eventually render that matrix as a sortable table:






WarningScaffold placeholder



The curriculum-map matrix (rows = course, columns = PLO 1-8, cells = I / R / M) is not yet built. The schema for outcomes/data/curriculum_map.yml plus a script to render it as the table above is queued. Each course’s mapping requires a structured conversation with the instructor, using the EESC 301 pilot as the template.




Version history









Version
Date
Outcomes
Notes




v2 (current)
28 April 2026
8 in 4 categories
Refined by Dr. Nelson Jatel, PAg, following student + faculty consultation and best-practice benchmarking. Memo: UBCO_Memo_PLOs_v2_20260428.docx.


v1 (draft)
2025
14 in 5 categories
Superseded by v2. Mapping of v1 → v2 in the memo §6 crosswalk. Draft: BSc Freshwater Science Draft PLOs (V1).docx.





Assessment plan, pending
The v2 finalisation memo flags two further deliverables:

Direct assessment artifacts in EESC 301, EESC 402, and the capstone course, identifying which artifact assesses which PLO.
Three-year review cycle aligned with UBC institutional assessment guidance.

Both are pending and will be tracked in the project’s GitHub issues.


References

UBCO Freshwater Science Program. (2026). Finalisation of Freshwater Science Program Learning Outcomes (PLOs), v2. Internal memo, 28 April 2026. (docs/reference/learning-outcomes/UBCO_Memo_PLOs_v2_20260428.docx)
UBCO Freshwater Science Program. (2025). Draft Program Learning Outcomes (V1). Internal document. (docs/reference/learning-outcomes/BSc Freshwater Science Draft PLOs (V1).docx)
UBC Faculty of Science EECB program curriculum-map template (used as the basis for the I / R / M methodology and the EESC 301 worked example).
Penn State Office of Planning, Assessment and Institutional Research. Program Learning Objectives.
Washington State University Office of Assessment for Curricular Effectiveness. (2024). Quick Guide to Program-level Student Learning Outcomes.
University of Wisconsin-Milwaukee. Freshwater Sciences, BS, Program Learning Outcomes.

title: PAg, Professional Agrologist (BC Institute of Agrologists)
text: Other professional pathways: RPBio (College of Applied Biologists) · Complementary course bundles (Chemistry, Biology, Hydrology)

title: PAg, Professional Agrologist (BC Institute of Agrologists)
text:
Academic requirements (AAg entry)
A four-year accredited B.Sc. with a science focus, where the coursework includes:

At least 8 entry-level foundational courses (100/200-level) in the subjects on BCIA’s Academic Worksheet.
At least 20 courses in agriculture, natural sciences, or agriculture/resource economics that relate to agrology under the Agrologists Regulation, 2021.
At least 8 senior-level courses (drawn from the 20 above) in those same subject areas.

Eligible course categories:

Agrology
Foundational Natural Science
Mathematics or Statistics
Economics
Communications / Writing
Computer Science

title: PAg, Professional Agrologist (BC Institute of Agrologists)
text:
What’s already covered

Foundations (8 required): Year 1 science (BIOL 116, CHEM 121 + CHEM 123, MATH 100, PHYS 111/112 paired with PHYS 121/122) plus EESC 205 (Hydrology) and EESC 222 (Geomorphology) cover the foundational natural-science count.
Communications / Writing: the program’s 6-credit English / communications block satisfies BCIA’s communications requirement.
Mathematics / Statistics: MATH 100 + STAT 230 / GEOG 271 / BIOL 202.

title: PAg, Professional Agrologist (BC Institute of Agrologists)
text:
BCIA-approved UBCO courses by category
The tables below render directly from the June-2025 BCIA UBCO approved course list (PDF), parsed into a YAML (data/designation_mappings/pag.yml) and committed to this repository. They update whenever BCIA republishes the list.

Foundational courses (8 required, 100/200-level)
48 UBCO courses are recognised in this category.








Agrology courses, 100/200 level
17 UBCO courses in the lower-level agrology category. These can count toward the 20-course agrology requirement (≥8 must be senior, listed below).








Agrology courses, 300/400+ level
85 UBCO senior-level agrology courses. BCIA requires ≥8 senior-level courses within the 20-course agrology total.

title: PAg, Professional Agrologist (BC Institute of Agrologists)
section: Sample 4-year plan for the PAg track
text:
Sample 4-year plan for the PAg track
Illustrative; verify with the program advisor and current BCIA rules.






NoteYear 1






T1: BIOL 116; CHEM 121; MATH 100; ENGL 109/112/114 (writing requirement); elective.
T2: BIOL 125; CHEM 123/121; PHYS 111 or 112; elective.










NoteYear 2






T1: EESC 205 (Hydrology); STAT 230 / GEOG 271 / BIOL 202; ECON 371 or 372 (example); elective.
T2: EESC 222 (Geomorphology); CHEM 211; CHEM 201 or 210 (example); elective.










NoteYear 3






T1: EESC 301 (Limnology); EESC 402 or other policy/management upper-level; GISC/GEOG 380/381 (GIS/Remote Sensing).
T2: Choose among EESC 333, 413, 422, 429, 431, 435; consider CHEM 301/302 as relevant.










NoteYear 4






Round out to ≥20 agrology-related courses with ≥8 at 3rd/4th-year across EESC, BIOL, CHEM, GEOG, GISC, ECON. Add a field or industry project (Directed Studies, Honours Thesis, or co-op term).

title: PAg, Professional Agrologist (BC Institute of Agrologists)
text:
Co-op and experience
Co-op terms after Year 2 or Year 3 target utilities, local and regional governments, environmental consulting, and agriculture / watershed NGOs. These count toward the post-degree articling experience required to convert AAg → PAg.

title: PAg, Professional Agrologist (BC Institute of Agrologists)
text:
Seat / prerequisite cautions

GIS courses (GISC 380/381) often have priority rules for majors. Request permission early.
ECON seats fill quickly; plan registration day.
Some upper-level EESC courses alternate years (313/413). Coordinate with the program advisor.

title: UBCO Freshwater Science
text:
NoteLand acknowledgement



The UBC Okanagan campus is situated within the unceded, ancestral, and traditional territory of the Syilx Okanagan Nation. The Freshwater Science program, its teaching, its research, its applied work in freshwater systems, depends on this land and on relationships with the Indigenous Peoples and rights-holders who continue to steward it.

title: UBCO Freshwater Science
text:
Quick starts


I’m a current student

The interactive Planner is in development; preview the design intent on its placeholder page.
Check the PAg pathway and RPBio pathway to see what gaps you still need to fill before graduation.
Browse the course catalogue for prerequisites and “is this offered next year?” signals.
The department’s official page lives at eesc.ok.ubc.ca → Freshwater Science.



I’m a prospective student

Read the official program description at UBC Undergraduate Admissions → Freshwater Science.
Skim the program overview below.
Compare the designation pathways to see what professional qualifications this degree can lead to.
Browse the first-year courses to get a feel for the workload.

title: UBCO Freshwater Science
text:
About the program
The Freshwater Science program integrates aquatic biology, chemistry, geography, and earth and environmental sciences. Students study water quality and quantity, aquatic organisms, and the health of aquatic ecosystems.
The four-year B.Sc. requires 120 credits with at least 78 in Science, at least 42 at the upper level (300+), and at least 36 of those upper-level credits in Science. The Honours track adds a research thesis (EESC_O 449).
Official UBCO pages:

UBC Undergraduate Admissions, Freshwater Science (program overview for prospective students)
Department of Earth, Environmental and Geographic Sciences, Freshwater Science (department page; current-student facing)
UBC Academic Calendar, Freshwater Science requirements (authoritative degree requirements)







NoteSource of truth



Course descriptions and prerequisites are pulled daily from the UBC Okanagan Academic Calendar. Designation requirements are curated from the BCIA UBCO Approved Course List and the CAB Credentialing Standard by the program lead. The Methodology page details the data pipeline and refresh cadence.

Source code is open and at github.com/DrNelsonJatel/UBCO_Freshwater_Science_Program. Issues and suggestions welcome via the GitHub issues tracker.

title: Complementary pathways
text:
Many students who pick Freshwater Science want a focus on top of the broad core, an applied direction they can take into work or graduate school. The patterns below come from the program lead’s curated advising map (docs/reference/advising/Map UBCO Freshwater Science (BSc) - Pathways.docx). Use them as starting points; the program advisor will sharpen the specific course choices to your goals.

Professional designation pathways: PAg (BC Institute of Agrologists) · RPBio (College of Applied Biologists)


Water quality and biogeochemistry
A Chemistry-leaning track that builds applied analytical and contaminant-cycling expertise.

Core EESC: EESC 301 (Limnology), EESC 309 (Global Biogeochemistry), EESC 423 (Tracers of Natural Processes).
Core CHEM: CHEM 211 (Analytical), CHEM 301 (Aqueous Environmental Chemistry).
GISC Minor to add spatial analysis and remote sensing.
Suits: contaminant monitoring, redox / speciation work, trace analysis in waters, water-utility lab roles.
Lab home: UBCO Chemistry FiLTER (Field Lab for Trace Element Research) is a frequent collaborator.



Aquatic ecology and conservation
A Biology-leaning track for biomonitoring, fisheries, and species-level work.

Core EESC: EESC 301 (Limnology), EESC 402 (Freshwater Resources).
Core BIOL: BIOL 308 (Population Biology), BIOL 375 / FWSC 375 (Flora & Fauna of Inland Waters).
Year-4 capstone: Directed Studies or EESC 449 (Honours Thesis).
Co-op targets: provincial and federal biology agencies, fisheries consulting, NGOs.
Designation fit: this is the natural pairing with the RPBio pathway.



Hydrology and water resources
A Civil-Engineering-adjacent track for watershed modelling, flood/drought risk, and water infrastructure.

Core EESC: EESC 413 (Analytical Methods in Hydrology), EESC 435 (Fluvial Field Techniques), EESC 423 (Tracers), EESC 342 (Hydrogeology).
ENGR pairing (advisory only): students with strong interest in the engineering side of hydrology sometimes pursue ENGR courses such as Open Channel Flow with permission from the School of Engineering. Seat and prerequisite limits favour Engineering majors, and ENGR courses are outside the scope of this planning resource (the planner does not track them). Coordinate any ENGR pursuit with the Engineering advising office directly.
GISC Minor for geospatial modelling.
Co-op targets: hydrometric agencies (ECCC, WSC), engineering consulting (NHC, Associated Engineering, etc.), utilities.



Practical notes for mixing and matching

Co-op across programs. Co-op is available in Freshwater, Chemistry, Biology, and Engineering, ideal for stitching together applied experience.
Credit double-counting. The UBCO Academic Calendar caps double-counting of upper-level credits toward a second major or minor (generally max 6 credits for program-specified requirements). Schedule intentionally if you’re aiming for a double credential.
Advising. Use the official Program Plan and meet program advisors early, especially if you’re eyeing Honours, co-op, or cross-faculty electives.



References

UBCO Freshwater Science (program page)
UBC Academic Calendar, Freshwater Science requirements
UBCO BSc Freshwater Science, Program Plan (PDF)
UBCO Chemistry, undergraduate page
UBCO Civil Engineering, programs
UBCO GISC Minor
Source notes in this repo: docs/reference/advising/Map UBCO Freshwater Science (BSc) - Pathways.docx.

title: RPBio, Registered Professional Biologist (College of Applied Biologists)
text: Other professional pathways: PAg (BC Institute of Agrologists) · Complementary course bundles (Chemistry, Biology, Hydrology)

title: RPBio, Registered Professional Biologist (College of Applied Biologists)
text:
Academic requirements
A bachelor’s degree (or master’s) with:

25 science courses, 13 in biology.
Required core courses across:

Communications (typically first-year writing)
Chemistry
Numeracy (math)
Statistics at 2nd-year or higher
Applied biology / management
Ecology
Three of five from: genetics, cell biology, physiology, systematics/taxonomy, evolution








WarningKnown UBCO gap: systematics / taxonomy



UBCO does not currently offer a dedicated undergraduate course in systematics or taxonomy. Students targeting RPBio should either (a) plan to satisfy this requirement via a course taken at another institution and transferred in, or (b) cover the three-of-five via evolution + cell biology + genetics (or + physiology) instead. Confirm with the program advisor and CAB before registering. This gap was identified during the May 2026 QAQC pass against the live UBC Okanagan Academic Calendar.

title: RPBio, Registered Professional Biologist (College of Applied Biologists)
text:
What’s already covered

Communications: first-year writing block (ENGL or program-approved alternatives).
Chemistry: CHEM 121/123, 201/210/211, 301, comfortable coverage.
Numeracy: MATH 100/101.
Statistics (≥2nd-year): STAT 230 or GEOG 271 or BIOL 202.
Ecology / applied biology: BIOL 201 (Ecology & Evolution), BIOL 308 (Population Biology), FWSC/BIOL 375 (Flora & Fauna of Inland Waters).

title: RPBio, Registered Professional Biologist (College of Applied Biologists)
section: Required core areas + the three-of-five subject areas
text:
Required core areas + the three-of-five subject areas
The tables below render directly from data/designation_mappings/rpbio.yml, the curated mapping of CAB’s requirements onto UBCO courses, based on the CAB Credentialing Standard (June 2025) plus the program-lead’s advising pack (CAB does not publish a UBCO-specific approved-course PDF).

Required core areas
These six areas all need at least one qualifying UBCO course on the transcript.

Communications








Chemistry








Numeracy








Statistics (≥ 2nd-year)








Applied biology / management








Ecology









Three-of-five subject-area requirement
CAB requires at least three of these five subject areas to be covered. Evolution (BIOL 201) is already in the Freshwater Science core, so the practical target is “two more of genetics / cell biology / physiology / systematics”.

Genetics








Cell biology








Physiology








Systematics / taxonomy








Evolution









Biology-count check
CAB requires 13 biology courses. The planner uses this list of UBCO codes when computing your biology-count coverage:


**31 UBCO course codes** counted toward the 13-biology threshold:

`BIOL 116`  ·  `BIOL 117`  ·  `BIOL 122`  ·  `BIOL 125`  ·  `BIOL 200`  ·  `BIOL 201`  ·  `BIOL 202`  ·  `BIOL 204`  ·  `BIOL 205`  ·  `BIOL 209`  ·  `BIOL 210`  ·  `BIOL 228`  ·  `BIOL 265`  ·  `BIOL 306`  ·  `BIOL 308`  ·  `BIOL 311`  ·  `BIOL 319`  ·  `BIOL 354`  ·  `BIOL 356`  ·  `BIOL 358`  ·  `BIOL 363`  ·  `BIOL 364`  ·  `BIOL 366`  ·  `BIOL 375`  ·  `FWSC 375`  ·  `BIOL 414`  ·  `BIOL 417`  ·  `BIOL 459`  ·  `BIOL 468`  ·  `EESC 301`  ·  `EESC 402`

title: RPBio, Registered Professional Biologist (College of Applied Biologists)
section: Sample 4-year plan for the RPBio track
text:
Sample 4-year plan for the RPBio track
Illustrative; verify with the program advisor and current CAB rules.






NoteYear 1






T1: BIOL 116; CHEM 121; MATH 100; ENGL 109/112/114 (writing requirement); elective.
T2: BIOL 125; the other of CHEM 121/123; PHYS 111 or 112; elective.










NoteYear 2






T1: BIOL 201; STAT 230 / GEOG 271 / BIOL 202 (≥2nd-year stats); EESC 205 (Hydrology); elective.
T2: EESC 222 (Geomorphology); CHEM 211; BIOL 200 (Cell); elective.










NoteYear 3






T1: EESC 301 (Limnology); FWSC/BIOL 375 (Flora & Fauna of Inland Waters); CHEM 301; elective.
T2: BIOL 265 (Genetics); BIOL 356 or 358 (Physiology) or BIO upper-level toward the 13-biology count.










NoteYear 4






T1: EESC 402 (Freshwater Resources); pick 1-2 of EESC 309 / 323 / 423 (geochem requirement); BIO upper-level (reaching the 13-BIO total).
T2: EESC 342 (Hydrogeology) or EESC 413 (Analytical Methods); BIO upper-level; Directed Studies / Field / Special Topics.

title: RPBio, Registered Professional Biologist (College of Applied Biologists)
text:
Co-op and experience
Co-op terms after Year 2 (summer start) for 8-12 months, or after Year 3 summer. Target biomonitoring, fisheries, environmental consulting, and government. Experience accumulated during BIT counts toward the RPBio bridge.

title: RPBio, Registered Professional Biologist (College of Applied Biologists)
text:
Seat / prerequisite cautions

Some 3xx BIOL seats prioritise Biology majors. Request permission early.
BIOL 200, 265, 356, 358, 371 are the gap-filler electives, plan registration so they aren’t squeezed by required EESC courses.

title: RPBio, Registered Professional Biologist (College of Applied Biologists)
text:
Professional work products
To convert BIT → RPBio you must submit at least one scientific or risk-assessment report that meets College criteria, plus at least one public-facing communication piece (annotated presentation, briefing note, interpretation document, etc.). Directed Studies, Honours Thesis, and co-op term reports are the natural vehicles for these.

title: Skills scaffolding
text:
WarningScaffold placeholder



Skills are stored in skills/skills_vocab.yml (the controlled vocabulary) and skills/course_tags.yml (per-course tagging with introduce/practise/expect markers). Neither file exists yet. The heatmap viz on this page is the eventual rendering of those YAMLs.



The idea
Skills get scaffolded through a degree: introduced in one course, practised in several, and expected as background in upper-year courses. Without an explicit map, neither students nor instructors can see this arc clearly.
This page shows the scaffolded coverage as a heatmap: rows are skills from a controlled vocabulary, columns are the four years of the program, cells are coloured by introduce / practise / expect status.


Controlled skill vocabulary (draft)
Three top-level skill families to start, each with sub-skills. These are stubs; the real vocabulary will be co-developed with department instructors.


Quantitative / computational

R basics
R intermediate (tidyverse)
R for hydrology (tidyhydat, dataRetrieval)
Statistical inference
Geostatistics
Spatial analysis (sf, terra)
Modelling (GLM, GAM)



Field / lab

Sampling design
Water-quality sampling
Macroinvertebrate identification
Hydrometric measurement
Lab safety
Calibration and QC



Communication / professional

Scientific writing
Public-facing communication
Peer review
Project management
Ethics
Working with First Nations





Pending content

Co-develop the controlled skill vocabulary with the program instructor cohort.
Tag each Freshwater Science course with the skills it introduces / practises / expects.
Render the skills-by-year heatmap.

title: For Department Review
text:
This site is a curated advising and planning resource for the Bachelor of Science Freshwater Science major. It exists alongside , not in place of, the UBC Okanagan Academic Calendar, which remains the authoritative source for all degree requirements.
The site is intended for two audiences: current Freshwater Science students (the headline use case is the in-development interactive planner) and prospective students browsing the program.
I am sharing this with the Freshwater Science Program Committee and the Department of Earth, Environmental and Geographic Sciences to invite review of three specific judgement calls embedded in the material. Each is summarised below, with the relevant evidence and the section of the site where the curated content lives.

title: For Department Review
text:
1. The v2 Program Learning Outcomes
The site renders the v2 PLO list (eight outcomes in four categories) finalised in the 28 April 2026 memo, which consolidated the v1 draft of 14 outcomes following:

a student focus group (Freshwater Science majors),
a faculty working session, and
best-practice benchmarking (Penn State, WSU, CSUSB, SUNY, U Michigan-Dearborn, plus comparator BSc freshwater programs at UW-Milwaukee, Western Michigan, and UW-Madison).

The v1→v2 crosswalk and benchmarking evidence are in §4 and §6 of the memo, and the eight outcomes themselves are rendered verbatim on the Outcomes page.
Asks:

Is the eight-outcome v2 list the final version the Program Committee endorses for institutional submission, or should we iterate?
The curriculum-map matrix (per-course I/R/M coding for each PLO) is the next deliverable from the memo. The EESC 301 pilot mapping shows the methodology. We need a path to interview each Freshwater Science instructor (≈ 20-40 hours total). Can the department support this in fall 2026?

title: For Department Review
text:
2. The RPBio systematics-taxonomy gap
UBCO does not currently offer a dedicated undergraduate course in systematics or taxonomy. This is a real gap for Freshwater Science students targeting Registered Professional Biologist (RPBio) registration with the College of Applied Biology, because the June 2025 CAB Credentialing Standard lists systematics as one of the five subject areas from which RPBio candidates must hold at least three. The RPBio designation page carries a prominent callout-warning surfacing this gap and recommending that students either transfer in a systematics course from another institution or cover the three-of-five via evolution + cell biology + genetics instead.
Asks:

Should the department propose a UBCO course to close this gap (e.g., a 300-level “Biodiversity and Systematics” course shared with BIOL or EESC)? If yes, this advising site can become the forcing function for that proposal.
In the interim, is the callout-warning on the RPBio page an acceptable disclosure for students considering this designation, or do we want to soften / strengthen the language?

title: For Department Review
text:
3. The designation pathway recommendations
The site surfaces curated 4-year sample plans for two professional designations:

Professional Agrologist (PAg), BC Institute of Agrologists
Registered Professional Biologist (RPBio), College of Applied Biology

It deliberately does not surface pathways for:

Professional Geoscientist (P.Geo), EGBC. The Environmental Geoscience track is technically viable but cross-loaded with compulsory geoscience courses (mineralogy, sed/strat, structural, geophysics) that compete with the freshwater core. Decision: better signposted as a complementary pathway, not a headline track.
Professional Engineer (P.Eng), EGBC. A B.Sc. degree does not normally meet CEAB engineering academic standards; this is a second-degree path.

Both decisions were made by the program lead based on the advising-pack source document and on what we hear from students. The Complementary pathways page covers the GISC-Minor, Chemistry, Biology, and hydrology/water-resources course bundles that students can layer on top of the FW degree.
Asks:

Does the department agree with surfacing only PAg + RPBio as headline designation tracks?
The complementary-pathways page suggests three curated electives bundles, water quality + biogeochemistry, aquatic ecology, and hydrology + water resources. Are there additional bundles the department would surface, or wording on these we want to refine?
